content.problemStatement:
Given an array of NNN non-negative integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​. In one operation, you can increase **any one** of the elements by 111.

Find the **minimum** number of operations needed to make the XOR of all elements (A1⊕A2⊕…⊕ANA_1 \oplus A_2 \oplus \ldots \oplus A_NA1​⊕A2​⊕…⊕AN​) equal to 000. Here ⊕\oplus⊕ denotes the bitwise XOR operation.


Input Format
The first line of input contains one integer TTT - the number of test cases. Descriptions of TTT test cases follow.


The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN - the size of the array. The second line of each test case contains NNN integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ - the elements of the array.



Output Format
For each test case, print one number - the **minimum** number of operations required to make the array XOR equal to 000. It can be shown that it is always possible.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Increase A3A_3A3​ twice by using two operations. Thus, the array becomes [3,4,7][3, 4, 7][3,4,7]. The XOR of all elements of the array is 3⊕4⊕7=03\oplus 4\oplus 7 = 03⊕4⊕7=0. It can be shown that the XOR of all elements cannot be made equal to 000 using less than 222 operations.
Test case 222: Use one operation to increase A3A_3A3​ by 111. Thus, the array becomes [5,3,1,7][5, 3, 1, 7][5,3,1,7]. The XOR of all elements of the array is 5⊕3⊕1⊕7=05\oplus 3\oplus 1\oplus 7 = 05⊕3⊕1⊕7=0. It can be shown that the XOR of all elements cannot be made equal to 000 using less than 111 operation.
Test case 333: The XOR of all elements is already 000. Thus, we require 000 operations.
